content.text: Reproductive processes give rise to new individuals that are similar to their parents, but subtly different. Inheritance from the previous generation provides both a common basic body design and subtle changes in it for the next generation. As generations pass, both inherited differences and newly created differences accumulate, creating diversity.

content.title: Asexual vs Sexual Reproduction
content.text: In asexual reproduction (e.g., bacteria dividing), minor differences are generated only due to small inaccuracies in DNA copying, resulting in offspring that are very similar (e.g., a field of sugarcane). However, in sexual reproduction (e.g., human beings), the mechanism inherently maximises successful variations, leading to quite distinct and visible variations among different individuals.
Not all variations in a species have equal chances of surviving in a specific environment. Depending on the nature of variations, different individuals gain different kinds of advantages.

For example, bacteria that have developed an inherited variation allowing them to withstand heat will survive significantly better during a heat wave compared to normal bacteria. This selection of variants by environmental factors forms the crucial basis for evolutionary processes.

The most obvious outcome of the reproductive process is the generation of individuals of a similar design. The rules of heredity determine the exact process by which specific traits and characteristics are reliably inherited.

While a child bears all the fundamental features of a human being, it does not look exactly like its parents due to natural variations. Human populations natively exhibit a great deal of variation in visible inherited traits.

content.title: Observation of Earlobes as Inherited Traits
procedure: Observe the ears of all students in the class.
Prepare a list and calculate the percentage of students having completely 'free' earlobes versus closely 'attached' earlobes.
Correlate the specific earlobe type of each student with the earlobe types of their respective parents.
content.conclusion: Free and attached earlobes are two distinct, reliably inherited variants commonly found in human populations, following specific rules of genetic inheritance.

notes.section_title: 8.2.2 Rules for the Inheritance of Traits — Mendel's Contributions

content.text: The rules for the inheritance of traits in human beings and other sexually reproducing organisms rely on a fundamental fact: both the optimal male parent (father) and the female parent (mother) contribute practically equal amounts of genetic material (DNA) to the child.

This means that every single trait is potentially influenced by both paternal and maternal DNA. Consequently, for each individual trait, there will be two discrete versions (or copies) present in each child.

Gregor Johann Mendel, blending his knowledge of science and mathematics, mathematically worked out the main rules of inheritance using contrasting visible characters of garden peas—such as tall/short plants, round/wrinkled seeds, and white/violet flowers.

Mendel first crossed pure tall plants with pure short plants. In the first generation (F1 progeny), all plants were tall; there were absolutely no medium-height plants or halfway characteristics. Only one parental trait (tallness) was visibly expressed.

He then allowed these F1 tall plants to reproduce by self-pollination to generate the second generation (F2 progeny). Instead of all being tall, one-quarter (25%) of the F2 plants were short. This groundbreaking result indicated that the shortness trait was indeed inherited and present in the F1 plants, but it was just not expressed.

content.title: Genes (Factors)
content.text: Mendel proposed that two copies of controlling factors (now biologically called genes) are present in sexually reproducing organisms. These two copies may be identical (e.g., TT or tt) or different (e.g., Tt) depending exclusively on their precise parentage.

In Mendel's experiments, plants with the genetic makeup 'TT' and 'Tt' were both physically tall, while only 'tt' plants were physically short. This reveals that a single copy of 'T' is entirely sufficient to make the plant tall.

content.title: Definitions
content.text: Traits like 'T' (tallness), which express themselves completely even if only one copy is present, are termed 'dominant traits'. Conversely, traits like 't' (shortness), which require both copies to be identical to visibly express themselves, are termed 'recessive traits'.

content.title: Confirming the F2 Generation Ratio
procedure: To rigorously confirm that the F2 generation had a 1:2:1 genetic ratio of TT, Tt, and tt combinations despite showing a 3:1 physical ratio (Tall to Short), one must independently breed the individual F2 plants.

Self-pollinating a 'tt' plant will yield only short plants. Self-pollinating the tall plants will reveal their true genetic makeup: 'TT' plants will yield only tall offspring, whereas 'Tt' plants will again yield both tall and short offspring in a precise 3:1 physical ratio.

topics.topic_name: Independent Inheritance of Multiple Traits (Dihybrid Cross)

content.text: Mendel examined what happens when pea plants showing two entirely different characteristics are bred. He crossed a primarily tall plant bearing round seeds with a short plant bearing wrinkled seeds.
The resulting F1 progeny were all tall and all possessed round seeds, confirming that tallness and round seeds are both dominant traits.

When these F1 progeny were self-pollinated to produce F2 offspring, completely new combinations appeared. Alongside the original combinations (tall/round and short/wrinkled), some F2 offspring were tall but bore wrinkled seeds, while others were short but bore perfectly round seeds.

content.title: Conclusion
content.text: These brand new combinations of traits mathematically proved that the genes controlling plant height and the genes controlling seed shape are inherited completely independently of each other. They casually recombine during zygote formation.

notes.section_title: 8.2.3 How do these Traits get Expressed?

content.text: The mechanism of heredity relies on cellular DNA acting as the definitive information source for making specific proteins within the cell. A discrete section of DNA that provides the exact information for one specific protein is called the gene for that protein.

How do these proteins control traits like tallness? Plant height heavily depends on the amount of specific growth hormones. The amount of hormone naturally produced depends entirely on the efficiency of the biological process synthesising it, which is driven by specific enzymes (proteins).

If the gene for a growth enzyme is optimal, the enzyme works highly efficiently, lots of hormone is made, and the plant consequently grows tall. If that gene has an inherited alteration that makes the enzyme significantly less efficient, less hormone is produced, resulting in a physically short plant. Thus, genes directly control characteristics by managing protein production.

Since both parents inherently contribute equally to the progeny, each parent provides one complete copy of every gene. Thus, every normal body cell has two identical sets of all genes. To ensure genetic stability without endlessly multiplying DNA content, specialised germ-cells must only carry a single gene set.

These distinct gene sets are not present as a single massive thread of DNA. Instead, they exist as separate, independent physical pieces called chromosomes. Every normal cell possesses two paired copies of each structural chromosome (one paternal, one maternal).

content.title: Mechanism of Independent Inheritance
content.text: During germ-cell generation (meiosis), every gamete naturally takes merely one chromosome from each maternal/paternal pair. Because these chromosomes separate randomly and independently of each other, traits residing on different chromosomes (like tallness and round seeds) are independently inherited. Upon fertilisation, two combining germ-cells successfully restore the normal paired number of chromosomes in the progeny.
Different biological species use drastically different strategies to determine the sex of a newborn individual. Some species rely entirely on specific environmental cues.

content.title: Environmental Examples
items: Reptiles: In a few specific reptiles, the exact incubation temperature at which fertilised eggs are kept ultimately determines whether the developing animal will be physiologically male or female.
Snails: Certain individuals can literally change sex during their lifetime, definitively indicating that their sex is not rigidly determined by genetics.

content.text: Conversely, in humans, the biological sex of a newly formed individual is determined entirely genetically at the exact moment of fertilisation, depending on the specific genes inherited from the parents.

Most human chromosomes (22 pairs) exist as perfect pairs with a maternal and paternal copy. However, one specific pair, known as the sex chromosomes, determines biological gender and is odd because it does not always form a physically perfect pair.

content.title: Combinations
items: Females (XX): Women inherently possess a perfect pairing of sex chromosomes, with both chromosomes being normal-sized and called 'X'.
Males (XY): Men natively possess a mismatched pairing containing one normal-sized 'X' chromosome and one distinctive, short chromosome called 'Y'.

content.title: Determining the Sex of the Child
content.text: Because all females are XX, every single child strictly inherits an X chromosome from their mother. Therefore, the sex of the resultant child is determined entirely by what they inherit from their father. If the sperm carries an X chromosome, the child will be XX (a girl). If the fertilising sperm carries a Y chromosome, the child will be XY (a boy). Statistically, half the children will be boys, and half exactly will be girls.

glossary.definition: The biological process by which distinct traits and characteristics are reliably inherited from parents by their offspring.
The visible and subtle differences existing among the progeny or individuals of a species, originating inherently during reproduction.
A specific section of cellular DNA that provides the discrete information required for producing one specific functional protein, thereby directly controlling traits.
Separate independent, structured pieces of densely packed DNA occurring in pairs within cells; they physically house arrays of genes.
An inherited characteristic that visibly expresses itself fully in the progeny even if only a single copy of its associated gene is present (e.g., Tallness 'T').
An inherited characteristic that predictably remains hidden unless both matching copies of its associated gene are identical (e.g., Shortness 't').
The immediate first-generation offspring produced directly by deliberately crossing two drastically different purebred parental lines.
The subsequent second-generation offspring produced primarily by functionally self-pollinating or breeding the F1 progeny.
The distinct, specific pair of chromosomes (X and Y in humans) uniquely responsible for definitively determining the biological sex of a newborn individual.

summary_points: While asexual reproduction leads only to minor differences through DNA copying inaccuracies, sexual reproduction deliberately generates distinct, significant variations.
Variations arising naturally during the process of reproduction can be inherited by offspring.
Depending on the exact nature of the variations, they may critically lead to the increased survival capability of certain individuals under severe environmental stresses.

Mendel demonstrated that sexually reproducing individuals inherently possess two copies of genes for the identically same trait; the trait that visibly expresses over the other is called the dominant trait, while the hidden one is recessive.
Traits situated on different chromosomes in one individual are inherited entirely separately, biologically giving rise to brand new visual combinations of traits in the offspring.

Cellular DNA provides the exact functional blueprints to manufacture proteins; effectively, genes dictate specific characteristics by controlling the synthesis and efficiency of requisite structural and enzymatic proteins.
Biological sex is definitively determined by uniquely different mechanisms in various species (e.g., environmental cues like temperature in reptiles).

In human beings, the sex of the child exclusively biologically depends on whether the single paternal sex chromosome inherited via the sperm is structurally X (forming XX for girls) or physically Y (forming XY for boys).
